prompt: Compile the procurement/legal artifacts a kolm attestation must align with so it is ACCEPTED, not just produced, with primary-source specifics. Identify the standard vendor-onboarding documents enterprise procurement requires from a small AI vendor in 2026 and which a kolm Ed25519-signed Readiness Report can satisfy or accompany: the security questionnaire (covered in l2), plus the DPA/sub-processor list, the AI/model-use addendum, the vendor risk assessment / TPRM scorecard, SOC2/ISO mapping letters, and any 'AI vendor' rider now appearing in MSAs. Quote real 2026 examples of AI-specific procurement clauses or 'AI vendor security requirements' wording (URL or @handle+date), including any from regulated buyers (finance/health/gov). For each artifact, state whether kolm should (a) generate it, (b) map its report to it, or (c) stay out of scope. Conclude with the minimum artifact set kolm's report must reference to clear a typical enterprise procurement gate. Flag confidence; distinguish quoted clauses from inferred requirements.
content:
**Kolm (Ed25519-signed Readiness Report) primarily serves as a technical/security attestation and control-mapping artifact.** It aligns with (and can help satisfy or accompany) procurement requirements but does not replace legal/contractual documents, full audits, or buyer-specific risk scoring. Enterprise procurement in 2026 for small AI vendors typically requires a bundle of artifacts to clear gates; the Kolm report excels at evidence-backed mappings for security, AI-specific controls, and compliance posture but stays out of full legal drafting.[[1]](https://www.pwc.com/us/en/tech-effect/ai-analytics/responsible-ai-tprm.html)[[2]](https://www.atlassystems.com/blog/ai-vendor-risk-questionnaire)

### Standard Vendor-Onboarding Documents (2026 Context)
Enterprise buyers (especially in finance, healthcare, and government) standardize on these for AI/SaaS vendors. Requirements have evolved with AI-specific risks (model training/data use, bias/explainability, output liability, sub-processor AI chains). Key sources include TPRM frameworks, updated questionnaires (e.g., SIG with AI questions), and emerging AI addenda/riders.[[3]](https://mitratech.com/resource-hub/blog/third-party-risk-management-frameworks/)[[4]](https://www.ncontracts.com/nsight-blog/how-generative-ai-impacts-your-fis-risk-management-program)

- **Security Questionnaire** (often 100–800+ questions, including AI-specific sections on model governance, data provenance, training use, human oversight, and incident response). Frequently based on SIG, NIST AI RMF, or custom buyer templates. Covered in Kolm l2 (per query).  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map its report to it (e.g., control evidence, signed attestations on data handling/non-training). Often satisfies or pre-populates large portions.

- **DPA / Sub-Processor List** (Data Processing Addendum under GDPR/CCPA/etc., with mandatory sub-processor disclosure, notification rights, and flow-down obligations). Standard for any data-processing vendor; AI vendors must address model-provider sub-processors explicitly. Public lists and 30-day objection windows are common.[[5]](https://openai.com/policies/data-processing-addendum/)[[6]](https://www.hedy.ai/post/gdpr-checklist-ai-meeting-tools/)  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map/accompany (attest to data controls, sub-processor security posture, or non-use of customer data for training). Does not generate the full legal DPA.

- **AI/Model-Use Addendum** (standalone or annex addressing training/fine-tuning prohibitions, output ownership, explainability/human oversight, bias mitigation, and prohibited uses). Increasingly required as a rider or addendum to MSAs/SaaS agreements.[[7]](https://gc.ai/blog/ai-contract-review)[[8]](https://gouchevlaw.com/10-critical-clauses-for-ai-vendor-contracts/)  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map its report to it (evidence of controls aligning with addendum requirements, e.g., data segregation, logging, non-training attestations). Can support negotiation but does not draft the addendum.

- **Vendor Risk Assessment / TPRM Scorecard** (internal buyer scoring of inherent/residual risk, often with AI-specific criteria like model drift, data lineage, and regulatory alignment (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001)). Includes questionnaires + evidence review.[[2]](https://www.atlassystems.com/blog/ai-vendor-risk-questionnaire)[[3]](https://mitratech.com/resource-hub/blog/third-party-risk-management-frameworks/)  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map its report to it (provides structured, signed evidence for scoring categories like security, privacy, and AI governance).

- **SOC 2 / ISO Mapping Letters** (SOC 2 Type II report or bridge letters; ISO 27001; emerging ISO 42001 for AI management systems; mappings to buyer frameworks like NIST CSF or AI RMF). AI-focused extensions or attestations are increasingly requested.[[9]](https://www.knowlee.ai/blog/iso-42001-vs-soc2-vs-iso-27001-comparison)[[10]](https://www.isms.online/iso-42001/ai-vendor-risk/)  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map its report to them (or generate control mappings/attestations that complement SOC 2/ISO). Can reference or align with these for a combined package.

- **'AI Vendor' Rider in MSAs** (or equivalent addendum/rider in master agreements). These embed AI-specific terms (data ownership, training bans, "American AI" preferences in gov contexts, incident SLAs, IP in outputs). Commercial examples exist alongside gov mandates.[[11]](https://federalnewsnetwork.com/acquisition-policy/2026/03/gsas-new-ai-clause-drives-contractors-to-sound-the-alarm/)[[12]](https://www.crowell.com/en/insights/client-alerts/ai-for-government-7-days-for-contractor-comments-on-gsa-proposed-contract-clause-for-ai-systems)  
  **Kolm role**: (b) Map/accompany (evidence that controls satisfy rider requirements). Stays out of drafting the rider itself.

**Other common items** (often out of Kolm scope): Insurance certificates (with AI endorsements), business continuity plans, penetration test summaries, right-to-audit clauses, financial stability docs, and full MSA redlines.

### Quoted Real 2026 Examples of AI-Specific Clauses/Requirements
These are distinguished as **quoted** (primary or near-primary sources) vs. **inferred/summarized** from analyses. Focus is on 2025–2026 materials; commercial examples are less publicly verbatim than government ones due to NDA norms.

- **GSA Proposed GSAR 552.239-7001, "Basic Safeguarding of Artificial Intelligence Systems" (FEB 2026, GSAR Deviation)** — Primary gov example (proposed March 2026 for MAS contracts; comments extended; not finalized in Refresh 31). Applies to AI capabilities; takes precedence over commercial terms. Key quoted excerpts (from the PDF):  
  - Definitions include "American AI Systems," "Data Inputs/Outputs," "Government Data," "Large Language Model (LLM)," and "Custom Development."  
  - "(b) ... this clause ... controls" over conflicting commercial terms.  
  - "(d)(1)(i) The Government retains full ownership of ... all Government Data and Custom Developments."  
  - Prohibited uses of Government Data: "(i) Training, fine-tuning, or otherwise improving an LLM or other machine learning or AI models ... (ii) Targeting Government or non-Government entities or informing Contractor’s or Vendor’s advertising..."  
  - Data handling: "'eyes off' Data handling procedures"; logical segregation; deletion certification on termination.  
  - Other obligations: Disclosure of AI systems used; use only American AI Systems; human oversight/traceability (summarized intermediate steps, model routing, source attribution); 72-hour CISA incident reporting.[[13]](https://buy.gsa.gov/interact/system/files/GSA_Federal_Acquisition%20Service%20Proposed%20Government%20AI%20System%20Terms%20and%20Conditions.pdf)[[13]](https://buy.gsa.gov/interact/system/files/GSA_Federal_Acquisition%20Service%20Proposed%20Government%20AI%20System%20Terms%20and%20Conditions.pdf)  
  **URL**: https://buy.gsa.gov/interact/system/files/GSA_Federal_Acquisition%20Service%20Proposed%20Government%20AI%20System%20Terms%20and%20Conditions.pdf (or linked analyses). Applies to regulated gov buyers; influences commercial expectations.

- **PwC (June 2025, still relevant in 2026 analyses)**: "Revisit vendor contracts to encourage Responsible AI use. Update agreements to require disclosure when vendors use AI in service delivery. ... Scrutinize data usage policies. Confirm whether third parties are using your organization’s data to train AI models. ... Perform AI-specific due diligence ... consider adding AI-focused addenda to SOC 2 reports, independent attestations, or other governance tools."[[1]](https://www.pwc.com/us/en/tech-effect/ai-analytics/responsible-ai-tprm.html)[[1]](https://www.pwc.com/us/en/tech-effect/ai-analytics/responsible-ai-tprm.html) (Inferred/summarized guidance, not a verbatim contract clause.)

- **AI Vendor Questionnaires/TPRM (2026 examples)**: Common questions include compliance with "EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001"; model governance, data sources for training, explainability, and sub-processor AI use.[[2]](https://www.atlassystems.com/blog/ai-vendor-risk-questionnaire) (Template/inferred requirements; no single public verbatim clause.)

- **Commercial patterns (inferred from multiple 2026 sources)**: DPAs with explicit sub-processor lists and notification (e.g., OpenAI-style 30-day objection); guarantees against training on customer data; SOC 2 Type II + ISO 27001/42001 references; AI addenda covering output ownership and human oversight.[[5]](https://openai.com/policies/data-processing-addendum/)[[7]](https://gc.ai/blog/ai-contract-review)

No widespread public 2026 commercial MSA rider verbatim quotes were located (typical due to confidentiality); the GSA clause is the most detailed primary public example. Regulated sectors (finance/health/gov) emphasize data non-training, audit rights, and AI-specific risk scoring.[[4]](https://www.ncontracts.com/nsight-blog/how-generative-ai-impacts-your-fis-risk-management-program)

### Minimum Artifact Set Kolm's Report Must Reference to Clear a Typical Enterprise Procurement Gate
Kolm's signed Readiness Report should explicitly reference or map to at least: (1) security questionnaire alignment; (2) SOC 2/ISO (or equivalent) control mappings/attestations; (3) sub-processor/data-handling attestations (supporting DPA); (4) AI-specific controls (non-training of customer data, oversight mechanisms, incident processes) aligned to any addendum/rider; and (5) TPRM scorecard evidence categories. This package, combined with a buyer-completed questionnaire and basic certifications, typically clears initial gates for small AI vendors. Full legal docs (executed DPA, signed MSA rider) remain buyer-driven.

**Kolm generates** its own signed report and supporting mappings/attestations. It **maps** to questionnaires, scorecards, SOC/ISO, and AI addenda. It **stays out of scope** for drafting DPAs, full MSAs/riders, insurance, or non-security legal opinions.

**Confidence**: Medium (primary gov clause is strong; commercial 2026 verbatim rider examples are limited/inferred from guidance and patterns; real-world buyer templates vary). Requirements evolve quickly with regulations like EU AI Act deadlines.
